basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Quraysh — the name of the Prophet's tribe — makes with the preceding al-Fīl a tight diptych. The first two verses name an outward grace: the Quraysh caravans could travel in peace, in winter to Yemen, in summer to Syria, thanks to pacts of safety granted by the neighbouring powers. The two following verses draw the consequence: let them therefore worship the Lord of this House who has fed them and freed them from fear. But Qushayrī gives this logic a spiritual reach that lights up the whole sūra: “The meaning of the grace in food and security is that they may be free (yatafarraghū, يَتَفَرَّغُوا) for the worship of God — for he whose needs are not met cannot be free for obedience; neither his strength nor his heart will help him — save in security from every side.” And this is why, he adds, in the verse of trial (2:155), the Quran names fear before all other trials: fear paralyses even the very possibility of worship. The sūra therefore says, plainly, what every soul lives: God gives you bread and peace not to dull you, but to free you — so that your heart, having no more to seek, may seek Him.

versets.trad: For the secure passage of the Quraysh,
versets.commentaire: īlāf (إِيلَاف) comes from the root ʾ-l-f, which first names familiarity, habit — that to which one is bound, that which one is used to. The word came to designate the pacts of security by which Byzantium, Persia, Abyssinia, and Yemen granted the Quraysh free passage. And the verse opens with the causal preposition li-, “for”: it is by reason of this protected bond of habit that the sūra calls, in verse 3, for worship.

mots.def: preposition: for, by reason of.
īlāf: the bond of habit, familiarity; here, the pacts of security granted to the Quraysh.
Quraysh: name of the Prophet's tribe, guardian of the Kaʿba.

versets.trad: their secure passage on the journey of winter and of summer,
versets.commentaire: In winter toward Yemen for spices, in summer toward Syria for the products of the land. Two movements, and between them: the dwelling around the Kaʿba. An image of a life in rhythm — that goes out and comes back, that ventures and gathers itself. For the Sufi reader, the verse recalls that God is not only Lord of high states: He is Lord of seasons, roads, trade, ordinary life. And it is in that ordinary, and nowhere else, that He must be acknowledged.

mots.def: īlāf + suffix pronoun: their bond.
riḥla: journey, expedition, caravan.
shitāʾ: winter; the season of the caravan to Yemen.
conjunction: and.
ṣayf: summer; the season of the caravan to Syria.

versets.trad: so let them worship the Lord of this House,
versets.commentaire: hādhā l-bayt (هَٰذَا البَيْت), “this House” — the Kaʿba, the sanctuary the Quraysh are charged to keep. For Qushayrī the central teaching of the sūra is in the conjunction: because He has fed you and made you safe, you are free; and because you are free, worship. The outward grace does not conclude in comfort, it concludes in worship — otherwise it is lost. An inward reading reaches further: the Kaʿba is also a figure of the heart, the House where God dwells in the servant. All grace received in outward life has its meaning only as a means to return to this House — to worship its sole Lord there.

mots.def: linking particle: so, then.
command particle (lām al-amr): marks the 3rd person imperative.
ʿabada: to worship, to serve; jussive imperfect plural.
rabb: the Lord, the Master; in the construct accusative.
masc. sing. near demonstrative.
bayt: house; here “the House” par excellence — the Kaʿba; in Sufi reading, a figure of the servant's heart.

versets.ar: الَّذِي أَطْعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٍ وَآمَنَهُم مِّنْ خَوْفٍ
versets.tr: alladhī aṭʿamahum min jūʿin wa-āmanahum min khawf
versets.trad: who fed them in hunger and made them safe from fear.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “The meaning of the grace in food and security is that they may be free (yatafarraghū) for the worship of God. He whose needs are not met cannot be free for obedience; neither his strength nor his heart will help him, save in security from every side.” This is why, in the verse of trial (2:155), God names fear before hunger, and before every other suffering: fear paralyses the very possibility of worship. The sūra thus says plainly what every soul lives: God gives you bread and peace not to dull you, but to free you — so that your heart, having no more to seek, may seek Him.

mots.def: masc. sing. relative pronoun.
aṭʿama: to give to eat, to feed.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition: from, out of; here, “against hunger”.
jūʿ: hunger; one of the two fundamental evils that paralyse worship.
conjunction: and.
āmana: to make safe, to grant security.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: preposition.
khawf: fear, dread — what prevents the heart from gathering itself.
